coredata.dc:title: Signal-on electrochemical aptasensor for sensitive detection of sulfamethazine based on carbon quantum dots/tungsten disulfide nanocomposites
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yan
Gong, Chengju
Zhu, Yan
Wang, Qianqian
Geng, Liping
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, a novel electroactive material named as carbon quantum dots-tungsten disulfide nanocomposite (CQDs-WS2) was prepared via a facile self-assembly method. Based on CQDs-WS2 modified glassy carbon electrode (CQDs-WS2/GCE), a label-free electrochemical aptasensing strategy was proposed to sensitively determine sulfamethazine (SMZ). Cationic polyethyleneimine (PEI) were employed to form complexes with negatively charged SMZ-binding aptamers immobilized on CQDs-WS2/GCE by amidation reaction, which decreased the adsorption capacity of [Ru(NH3)6]3+ to aptamers. When SMZ existed, the change of aptamer conformation due to the formation of SMZ/aptamer complex would lead to decrease in adsorption amount of PEI. More adsorbed [Ru(NH3)6]3+ on aptamer can produce enhanced reduction current. On the basis of CQDs-WS2 as electroactive materials, aptamer as a recognition receptor, PEI as electrostatic adsorbent of aptamer, and [Ru(NH3)6]3+ as an electroactive indicator, a signal-on electrochemical aptasensor was constructed for detection of SMZ with high sensitivity. The SMZ aptasensor displayed wide linear response in the 10 pM to 1.0 µM concentration range and a low detection limit of 4.0 pM. The method can be applied to quantitation analysis of antibiotic residue in food and environmental samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemical aptasensor
Tungsten disulfide nanosheet
Carbon quantum dots
Polyethyleneimine
Antibiotic

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optimization of experimental conditions for electrochemical detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of stepwise fabrication and detection principle.
TEM images of (A) WS2 nanosheet, (B) CQDs and (C) CQDs-WS2 nanohybrid.
XRD spectra (A) and FT-IR patterns (B): (a) WS2 nanosheet, (b) CQDs, (c) CQDs-WS2 nanohybrid . (C) XPS spectra of CQDs-WS2.
EIS analysis of stepwise prepared electrodes: (a) bare GCE, (b) CQDs-WS2/GCE, (c) AP/CQDs-WS2/GCE, (d) MCH/AP/CQDs-WS2/GCE, (e) SMZ/MCH/AP /CQDs-WS2/GCE.

Influence of volume fraction of PEI (A) and RuHex concentration (B) on aptasensor response. The DPV response of the aptamer sensor towards 0.05 μM SMZ and background current were showed in red and black, respectively. The illustration in figure A displayed relationship between signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) and volume fraction of PEI. The illustration in figure B displayed relationship between signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) and RuHex concentration.(For interpretation of the references to colour in their figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article)

(A) DPV response to the target SMZ at a series of concentrations: (a):0, (b):0.01, (c): 0.05, (d): 0.1, (e):0.5, (f):1.0, (g):5.0, (h): 10.0 (i):50.0, (j):100.0 (k):500.0, (l):1000.0 nM. (B) The linear calibration plots.
Specificity of the aptasensor towards target analyte . Target SMZ: 50 nM; Other interferential antibiotics: 500 nM.
Reports using electrochemical method for SMZ determination.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Results of recovery test for SMZ detection in pork samples (n = 3).

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: The category of the paper is electrochemical aptasensor.

head.ce:title: Signal-on electrochemical aptasensor for sensitive detection of sulfamethazine based on carbon quantum dots/tungsten disulfide nanocomposites

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Materials Science, Collaborative Innovation Center of Functionalized Probes for Chemical Imaging in Universities of Shandong, Key Laboratory of Molecular and Nano Probes, Ministry of Education, Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Clean Production of Fine Chemicals, Shandong Normal University, Jinan 250014, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Collaborative Innovation Center of Functionalized Probes for Chemical Imaging in Universities of Shandong
Key Laboratory of Molecular and Nano Probes
Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Clean Production of Fine Chemicals

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Materials Science, Collaborative Innovation Center of Functionalized Probes for Chemical Imaging in Universities of Shandong, Key Laboratory of Molecular and Nano Probes, Ministry of Education, Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Clean Production of Fine Chemicals, Shandong Normal University, Jinan 250014, People's Republic of China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, a novel electroactive material named as carbon quantum dots-tungsten disulfide nanocomposite (CQDs-WS2) was prepared via a facile self-assembly method. Based on CQDs-WS2 modified glassy carbon electrode (CQDs-WS2/GCE), a label-free electrochemical aptasensing strategy was proposed to sensitively determine sulfamethazine (SMZ). Cationic polyethyleneimine (PEI) were employed to form complexes with negatively charged SMZ-binding aptamers immobilized on CQDs-WS2/GCE by amidation reaction, which decreased the adsorption capacity of [Ru(NH3)6]3+ to aptamers. When SMZ existed, the change of aptamer conformation due to the formation of SMZ/aptamer complex would lead to decrease in adsorption amount of PEI. More adsorbed [Ru(NH3)6]3+ on aptamer can produce enhanced reduction current. On the basis of CQDs-WS2 as electroactive materials, aptamer as a recognition receptor, PEI as electrostatic adsorbent of aptamer, and [Ru(NH3)6]3+ as an electroactive indicator, a signal-on electrochemical aptasensor was constructed for detection of SMZ with high sensitivity. The SMZ aptasensor displayed wide linear response in the 10 pM to 1.0 µM concentration range and a low detection limit of 4.0 pM. The method can be applied to quantitation analysis of antibiotic residue in food and environmental samples.

A novel label-free and signal-on electrochemical aptasensor based on carbon quantum dots-tungsten disulfide nanocomposite has been fabricated and used for the determination of sulfamethazine with good selectivity and high sensitivity.
Image, graphical abstract

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, problems associated with residues of antibiotics in foods of animal origin and environmental water source have been a major concern to people. As veterinary drugs, overused antibiotics can enter and accumulate in human body though the food and drinking water, which would lead to potential human health hazards including hypersensitive reaction, antibiotic resistance and toxic effect. Therefore, the maximum residue limits (MRL) of antibiotics in animal food have been legally established in many countries [1,2]. Sulfamethazine (SMZ) is one kind of sulfonamides antibiotic, the MRL of SMZ in animal products is set as 100 ng/g by Canada and China [3,4]. Thus, developing sensitive analytical methods for SMZ residue detection has become popular research topics in the field of food safety assessment and environmental contaminant monitoring. Compared with various analytical techniques (spectrophotometric [5,6], chromatographic [7,8], electrophoretic [9,10], and electrochemical techniques [11–14]) reported for trace SMZ analysis, electrochemical aptasensor showed some unique advantages such as good selectivity, low cost, rapid response and low detection limits. Aptamer-based electrochemical biosensor not only possesses high sensitivity resulting from electrochemical analysis, but also displays good specificity benefiting from high-affinity target -binding aptamer. Hence, various electrochemical aptasensors have been designed recently for various analytical applications [15–18]. However, only a few electrochemical aptasensors have been reported in literature for the detection of SMZ. He's group constructed an SMZ biosensor on the basis of Ag@Au core-shell nanoparticles modified Au electrode combined with ferrocene-labeled aptamer. This method needs expensive and complex labeling process and has a detection limit of 0.11 nM [19]. Herein, a new label-free aptasensing strategy was proposed, which provided a fast, selective and facile voltammetric method to detect trace amount of SMZ down to 4.0 pM.

Recently, various nanomaterials have been used as electroactive materials to amplify response signal and improve sensitivity of electrochemical aptasensor. As one of the newly emerging two-dimensional (2D) layered nanomaterials, WS2 nanosheets exhibit extraordinary properties including large electroactive surface area, more catalytic activity sites, less toxic and high intrinsic conductivity, implying that WS2 is excellent electroactive material to fabricate electrochemical aptasensing platform [20,21]. However, the poor water dispersion of WS2 nanosheets affects their further application. Therefore, scientists have tried to construct WS2- based hybrid nanostructure in order to improve dispersion properties and electronic conductivity [22,23]. As a new star in the carbon-nanomaterial family, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are zero-dimensional (0D) nanomaterials (less than 10 nm in size). Because CQDs have extraordinary characteristics, including hydrophilicity, biocompatibility, good chemistry stability and high stable photoluminescence, they possess potential applications in optoelectronic use, drug delivery and bioimaging [24–27]. However, as far as we know, CQDs-WS2 nanocomposites have never been synthesized and applied to prepare electrochemical sensors in the published literature. In this paper, we constructed a new type of 0D/2D heterojunction composed of CQDs and WS2 nanosheets using a facile self-assembly method. The obtained CQDs -WS2 nanomaterial exhibited synergistically enhanced electrochemical properties than pristine WS2 because heterostructure based on uniform distribution of the CQDs on WS2 nanosheets surface can enlarge specific surface area, accelerate electron transfer between WS2 and CQDs through its interconnected conducting bridge, increase water dispersion property and enhance conductivity. Hence, we designed a novel electrochemical aptasensing platform based on CQDs-WS2 nanocomposite modified glass carbon electrode in this work.

In recent years, water-soluble cationic polymers have been used to DNA sequence detection because the cationic polymer can combine with the phosphate group on single-stranded DNA by electrostatic interaction to form complexes with analogue double-stranded structure [28–31]. Based on the above principle, we designed a novel signal-on electrochemical aptasensor for voltammetric measurement of SMZ. As shown in Scheme 1 , CQDs-WS2 nanocomposite modified glass carbon electrode (CQDs-WS2/GCE) acted as the electrochemical sensing matrix to covalently immobilize SMZ-binding aptamers by the EDC/NHS activated amidation reaction between NH2 groups of the aptamer and carbonyl group of CQDs. Afterwards, the cationic polyethyleneimine (PEI) were employed to form complexes with negatively charged SMZ-binding aptamers, which decreased the adsorption capacity of [Ru(NH3)6]3+ (RuHex) to aptamers due to the anionic charge of aptamer neutralized by PEI. When SMZ existed, the formation of SMZ/aptamer complex can lead to the change of aptamer conformation, which would decrease in adsorption amount of PEI on aptamer. Therefore, more RuHex adsorbed on aptamer can produce enhanced reduction current. On the basis of CQDs-WS2 as electroactive materials, aptamer as a recognition receptor, PEI as electrostatic adsorbent of aptamer, and RuHex as an electroactive indicator, a signal-on electrochemical aptasensor was constructed for detection of SMZ with good selectivity and high sensitivity. The experimental results confirm that the proposed electrochemical aptasensing strategy provides a simple, reliable and effective means of biosensing trace SMZ residue for food safety detection and environmental contaminant monitoring application.

Sulfamethazine (SMZ), sulfathiazole, sulfadimethoxine, sulfadiazine and 6-mercapto-1-hexanol (MCH) were received from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Chloramphenicol, kanamycin, urea, citric acid, and polyethyleneimine (PEI, Branched, and M.W. 70,000, 50% W/V aqueous solution) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Hexaammineruthenium (III) chloride ([Ru(NH3)6]3+), carbodiimide (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, USA). Tungsten disulfide nanosheet was received from Nanjing XFNANO Materials Tech Co. Ltd. (Nanjing, China). Amino-functionalized SMZ binding aptamer was obtained d from Shanghai Sangon Biotech Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China) with the following sequences: 5’-NH2-TTA GCT TAT GCG TTG GCC GGG ATA AGG ATC CAG CCG TTG TAG ATT TGC GTT CTA ACT CTC -3’.

The high resolution transmission electron microscopy (JEM-2100, Tokyo, Japan) was used to measure transmission electron micrographs (TEM). X-ray diffraction patterns were recorded by X-ray diffractometer (D8 advance, Bruker AXS, Germany). Fourier transform infrared spectra were taken on an Alpha FT-IR spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and differential pulse voltammetric measurements were employed with three electrodes on a LK3200A electrochemical workstation (LANLIKE, Tianjin, China).

CQDs were synthesized according to the previous report [32]. The experimental details in synthesis process were offered in the Supplementary Material. The self-assembly method was performed to fabricate CQDs-WS2 nanocomposites. After the CQDs (5 g L−1) and WS2 (5 g L−1) aqueous solution were mixed and stirred ultrasonically for 60 min, the suspension was centrifuged to collect the black precipitates. Then, the CQDs-WS2 solid powder was obtained after dried at 60 °C.

The bare glassy carbon disk electrode (GCE, 3.0 mm diameter) was accordingly cleaned by polished, ultrasonically washed and dried with nitrogen gas. Then, 5 μL of homogenous CQDs-WS2 dispersion (1 mg mL−1) was coated on the surface of bare GCE. After dried at room temperature, CQDs-WS2/GCE was obtained. The aptamer was immobilized on CQDs-WS2/GCE by amide-bond between amino-modified aptamer and carbonyl group of CQDs. Initially, the electrode was immersed in a mixture of 20 mM EDC and 10 mM NHS for 2 h at room temperature to activate the carboxylic groups of CQDs. Afterward, 5 μL of 4.0 μM aptamer was dropped on CQDs-WS2/GCE surface and kept at 4 °C for 12 h in order to immobilize the aptamer covalently. After the obtained AP/CQDs-WS2/GCE was thoroughly rinsed with Tris-EDTA buffer, it was coated with 5 μL of 1 mM MCH solution for 60 min to avoid nonspecific adsorption and then washed with the phosphate buffer. Finally, a novel electrochemical aptasensor taken as MCH/AP/CQDs-WS2/GCE was successfully fabricated for further use. (Scheme 1)

The prepared aptasensor was incubated with desired concentrations of SMZ for 1 h at room temperature. After that, 5 μL of PEI with a volume fraction of 1% was dropped on SMZ/MCH/AP/CQDs-WS2/GCE for 60 min. Finally, the PEI/SMZ/MCH/AP/CQDs-WS2/GCE was washed with the phosphate buffer, which was applied to electrochemical detection immediately by differential pulse voltammetric (DPV) method. DPV measurements were performed with three electrodes in 0.01 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.0) containing 10 μM RuHex which was thoroughly deoxygenated by bubbling with high-purity nitrogen for 30 min. The DPV experiments parameters were selected as follows: sweep potential from +0.0 V to −0.6 V, 30 mV pulse amplitude, and pulse width of 0.1 s. The EIS measurements were carried out in 0.1 M KCl solution containing 5.0 mM K3Fe(CN)6/K4Fe(CN)6 with the frequency range from 0.1 Hz to 10 kHz.

The pork was purchased and assumed to be SMZ-free sample. SMZ standard solutions in appropriate amounts were added into 0.5 g of chopped and homogenized pork samples. Then, the spiked sample was ultrasonic extracted in 1 mL ethyl acetate for 10 min. After centrifuged at 2500 g for 10 min, the collected supernatant was dried under gentle nitrogen flow at 40 °C. Finally, the final precipitate was dissolved in 10 mL phosphate buffer (0.01 M, pH 7.0) to obtain sample solution.

The TEM images of pristine WS2, pure CQDs and CQDs-WS2 nanocomposite are displayed in Fig. 1 A–C. As can be seen in Fig. 1A, pure WS2 presented well-ordered thin-layer flake structure with the average size of 20–500 nm and thickness of 1–8 nm, indicating it was graphene-analogous two-dimensional few-layer nanosheets. The as-prepared CQDs are homogeneous spherical shape with diameter averaging about 2 nm (Fig. 1B), displaying high stability and good monodispersity in water attributing to rich carboxyl groups on CQDs surface.

Fig. 1C revealed the TEM image of CQDs-WS2, which showed that CQDs are well dispersed on WS2 nanosheets via surface charge driven assembly, indicating the formation of 0D/2D hybrid nanostructures due to electrostatic interaction between positively charged WS2 and negatively charged CQDs [22,32]. Compared with pristine WS2 nanosheets, CQDs-WS2 heterostructure avoided usual stacking and aggregation because the loading of water-dispersible CQDs can enlarge the specific surface area and enhance dispersing performance of the hybrid, confirming that CQDs-WS2 nanocomposite is excellent electroactive material to fabricate electrochemical aptasensing platform.

The composition and structure of as-prepared materials were further studied by X-Ray diffraction (XRD). As shown in Fig. 2 A, all diffraction peaks in the XRD pattern of WS2 nanosheets (curve a) are consistent with the hexagonal phase of WS2 (JCPDS No. 87-2417) [33,34]. The XRD pattern of the synthesized CQDs shows a board peak located at 27.3° (curve b), indicating the amorphous and disordered nature of carbon atoms [27]. After loading CQDs, the XRD spectra of CQDs-WS2 heterostructure (curve c) maintains the characteristic peaks of pristine WS2 due to the amorphous state of CQDs.

In order to further confirm the existence of CQDs on the WS2 nanosheets, the FT-IR spectrum of WS2 nanosheets, CQDs and CQDs-WS2 nanocomposites were recorded and displayed in Fig. 2B. In curve a, the infrared absorption peaks at 438, 624, 981 and 1120 cm -1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of the W-S bond and S-S bond [35]. For CQDs, the absorption peaks at 1401 cm−1 (C-N), 1606 cm−1 (N-H), 1664 cm−1 (C=O) and 3215 cm−1 (-OH) are shown in curve b, which revealed that the synthesized CQDs contain plentiful surface functional groups including amino groups (-NH 2) and carboxyl groups (-COOH) [36]. The FT-IR spectrum of CQDs-WS2 in curve c displayed both the infrared characteristic absorption peak of WS2 and CQDs, which confirmed that CQDs nanoparticles were successfully loaded on the surface of WS2 nanosheets. The compositions of elements in CQDs-WS2 nanocomposites were evaluated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The survey XPS spectra of CQDs-WS2 in Fig. 2C verified the presence of W, S, C, N, and O elements in CQDs-WS2 hybrid, which was well in accordance with the results in FT-IR spectrum, indicating the successful preparation of CQDs-WS2 nanocomposites.

To further study the electrochemical performance of CQDs-WS2 hybrid and aptasensor, we performed cyclic voltammetry measurements to evaluate electron transfer efficiency of different electrodes with K3 Fe(CN)6 as redox probe. The experiment results (Fig. S1) showed that the increased signal response of redox probe was recorded by using CQDs-WS2/GCE in comparison to WS2/GCE, indicating that the obtained CQDs-WS2 nanomaterial exhibited synergistically enhanced electrochemical properties than pristine WS2 because heterostructure based on uniform distribution of the CQDs on WS2 nanosheets surface can enlarge specific surface area and accelerate electron transfer. Therefore, CQDs-WS2 nanocomposites can not only introduce carboxyl group for aptamer immobilization but also improve the electron-transfer ability, which exhibits significantly enhanced electrocatalytic properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of experimental conditions for electrochemical detection
ce:section.ce:para: Because the doping amount of PEI was a vital influence factor for the sensitivity of aptasensor, the volume fraction of PEI was optimized. As shown in Fig. 4 A, both current response of the aptasensor towards 0.05 μM SMZ and background current in pH 7.0 phosphate buffer decreased with increasing volume fraction from 0.1 to 10%.The signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) reached the maximum when 1% PEI was dropped on aptasensor . Thus, 1% PEI was selected as the optimal doping amount in this research. In this label-free proposal, the concentration of electroactive indicator can greatly affect signal readout. As displayed in Fig. 4B, the current response of the aptasensor to 0.05 μM SMZ increased with the increase of RuHex concentration in the range of 5–500 μM. The S/N possessed the highest value when the concentration increases to 10 μM. Therefore, 10 μM of RuHex was chosen to be the optimized amount.

Optimization of other experimental parameters was also investigated. The respective figures and data in the Supplementary Material revealed the following optimized parameters: pH = 7.0 phosphate buffer, 4.0 μM of immobilized aptamer and 60 min of SMZ binding time.

The detection principle was as follows: In the absence of the target SMZ, phosphate backbone of the aptamer can adsorb lots of positively charged PEI, which lead to great decrease in adsorption amount of RuHex on aptasensor surface. Therefore, low background current was obtained. However, in the presence of the target SMZ, conformational change of aptamer induced by the formation of aptamer-SMZ complex can lead to decrease in adsorption amount of PEI on aptamer, resulting in more cationic RuHex adsorbed on aptamer which can produce enhanced reduction peak current. On the basis of recording current signal of the aptasensor in phosphate buffer solution containing the optimized amount of electroactive indicator RuHex after incubation of SMZ, a signal-on electrochemical detection strategy was realized for SMZ measurement.

The analytical performance of aptasensor was evaluated by measuring current response to the target SMZ at a series of concentrations using differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) technique under the optimal conditions. Fig. 5 A presents that the DPV signals increased with increasing the SMZ concentration. The peak current is linear in the 10 pM to 1.0 µM SMZ concentration range (Fig. 5B), with a regression equation of I (μA) = 5.127 + 1.056 lg [c] (nM) (R = 0.9986). The limit of detection (S/N = 3) was estimated to be 4.0 pM. Table 1 showed the comparison of this method with other reported methods for SMZ determination, demonstrating superiority of the turn-on strategy in terms of wide linear range and high sensitivity credited to the excellent signal amplification effect of CQDs-WS2 nanocomposite. In summary, the designed aptasensor provides certain advantages compared with other electrochemical sensors used to detect SMZ in the literature: (a) excellent selectivity, (b) ultralow detection limit, (c) facile label-free fabrication process, (d) fast response and (f) satisfactory long-term stability.

The specificity of the aptasensor to its target analyte was assessed by interference experiment. In this test, the interference effect of non-target antibiotics including sulfathiazole (STZ), sulfadimethoxine (SDM), sulfadiazine (SDZ) chloramphenicol (CAP), and kanamycin (KAN) on SMZ detection was studied by comparing current change (ΔI = I − I 0, where I is current signal of the aptasensor toward analyte, I0 is background signal) of target SMZ with that of various interferent species under the same experimental condition. The results in Fig. 6 showed that the peak current evidently increased in the presence of 50 nM SMZ (ΔI = 4.13 µA). In contrast, the peak current increased slightly after binding of 500 nM non-target antibiotics (ΔI < 0.2 µA for five interferent species). It was obviously proved that the aptasensor exhibits high selectivity for the discrimination of target SMZ.

Five independently prepared aptasensors were used to detect 5.0 nM SMZ for the purpose of examining the reproducibility. The current measurement provided a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 4.23%, suggesting good reproducibility for SMZ assay. The repeatability of the aptasensor was investigated by performing 10 consecutive measurements using one same aptasensor. The RSD is measured to be 3.56%, implying desirable stability of current response. The stability of CQDs-WS2 nanocomposites after anchoring of aptamers on CQDs-WS2 nanocomposites modified electrode was estimated as follows: DPV current response of five independently electrochemical aptasensors (MCH/AP/CQDs-WS2/GCE) prepared under the same conditions was recorded in 0.01 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.0) containing 10 µM RuHex, respectively. The RSD of current value was calculated as 3.81%, indicating that the fabricated CQDs-WS2 modified electrode had acceptable immobilization stability after anchoring of aptamers on CQDs-WS2/GCE. Results of storage stability tests showed that the same aptasensor stored at 4 °C for 14 days can retain 95% of its initial response towards 5 nM of SMZ, confirming that the constructed aptasensor possessed superior long-term stability.

The recoveries of SMZ in spiked pork samples were determined to evaluate the method reliability on actual samples detection. The results was obtained by the standard addition method and displayed in Table 2 . It can be seen that the average recoveries for three spiked samples have ranged in 98.1–100.2 %, confirming that the proposed strategy can meet the requirements for complex food samples analysis.

On the basis of carbon quantum dots/tungsten disulfide nanosheet nanohybrids as novel electroactive materials, aptamer as a recognition receptor, cationic polyethyleneimine as electrostatic adsorbent of aptamer, and [Ru(NH3)6]3+ as an electroactive indicator, a signal-on electrochemical aptasensor was constructed for sensitive detection of SMZ. Although the reproducibility of the aptasensor need to be farther improved, the designed aptasensor offered advantages including superior sensitivity, good stability, excellent selectivity, low cost and rapid response. In this label-free proposal, [Ru(NH3)6]3+ adsorbed directly on aptamer can produce reduction current signals, avoiding complicated aptamer premodification and high cost, which afforded simple and useful electrochemical technique for biosensing trace antibiotics residue in food safety detection.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflict of interest.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.electacta.2021.139054.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Analytical strategies for residue analysis of veterinary drugs and growth-promoting agents in food-producing animals: a review
J. Chromatogr. A

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.A.M. Stolker, U.A.T. Brinkman, Analytical strategies for residue analysis of veterinary drugs and growth-promoting agents in food-producing animals: a review, J. Chromatogr. A, 1067 (2005) 15–53.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photoelectrochemical aptasensing of ofloxacin based on the use of a TiO2 nanotube array co-sensitized with a nanocomposite prepared from polydopamine and Ag2S nanoparticles
Microchim. Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.F. Qin, L.P. Geng, Q.Q. Wang, Y. Wang, Photoelectrochemical aptasensing of ofloxacin based on the use of a TiO2 nanotube array co-sensitized with a nanocomposite prepared from polydopamine and Ag2S nanoparticles, Microchim. Acta, 186 (2019) 430.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Residual veterinary antibiotics in pig excreta after oral administration of sulfonamides
Environ. Geochem. Health

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.R. Qiu, T. Zhao, Q.Y. Liu, J.H. He, D.C. He, G.Y. Wu, Residual veterinary antibiotics in pig excreta after oral administration of sulfonamides, Environ. Geochem. Health, 38 (2016) 549–556.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The veterinary antibiotic journey: comparing the behavior of sulfadiazine, sulfamethazine, sulfamethoxazole and tetracycline in cow excrement and two soils
J. Soils Sediments

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Bailey, A. Spielmeyer, G. Hamscher, H. Schuttrumpf, R.M. Frings, The veterinary antibiotic journey: comparing the behavior of sulfadiazine, sulfamethazine, sulfamethoxazole and tetracycline in cow excrement and two soils, J. Soils Sediments, 16 (2016) 1690–1704.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chemiluminescence resonance energy transfer competitive immunoassay employing hapten-functionalized quantum dots for the detection of sulfamethazine
ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.F. Ma, K. Wen, R.C. Beier, S.A. Eremin, C.L. Li, S.X. Zhang, Chemiluminescence resonance energy transfer competitive immunoassay employing hapten-functionalized quantum dots for the detection of sulfamethazine, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces, 8 (2016) 17745–17750.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Portable and reusable sulfadimidine in dairy products
Sensors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.J. Hao, X.H. Zhou, Y. Zhang, F. Long, L. Song, H.C. Shi, Portable and reusable sulfadimidine in dairy products, Sensors, 15 (2015) 8302–8313.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Towards the determination of sulfonamides in meat samples: a magnetic and mesoporous metal-organic framework as an efficient sorbent for magnetic solid phase extraction combined with highperformance liquid chromatography
J. Chromatogr. A

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Xia, L.J. Liu, X.X. Lv, F. Qu, G.L. Li, J.M. You, Towards the determination of sulfonamides in meat samples: a magnetic and mesoporous metal-organic framework as an efficient sorbent for magnetic solid phase extraction combined with highperformance liquid chromatography, J. Chromatogr. A, 1500 (2017) 24–31.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of sulfonamide residues in chicken liver using highperformance liquid chromatography
Food Anal. Methods

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K.J. Deng, X.H. Lan, G. Sun, L.Y. Ji, X.L. Zheng, Determination of sulfonamide residues in chicken liver using highperformance liquid chromatography, Food Anal. Methods, 9 (2016) 3337–3344.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of sulfametoxazole, sulfadiazine and associated compounds in phar-maceutical preparations by capillary zone electrophoresis
J. Chromatogr. A
